Event: Ned overprotects and shelters Rod and Todd
Explanation: He gains a sense of control but stunts his sons’ social development and emotional health.

Event: Rod and Todd plan to lose their virginities to each other due to their vow of abstinence
Explanation: Their plan reflects deep confusion and would seriously harm themselves without benefiting anyone.

Event: Marge gives Rod and Todd more freedom than Ned does
Explanation: She gains warm feelings and gratitude while improving the boys’ happiness and autonomy.

Event: Ned disowns Todd for having a crisis of faith
Explanation: He damages his relationship with his son and Todd’s well‑being while gaining only self-righteousness and later regret.

Event: Ned prays for God to punish Todd for lack of faith
Explanation: He seeks supernatural harm on his child, feeding his own zeal while worsening Todd’s emotional situation.

Event: Ned sends Todd to live with the Simpsons so they’ll make him afraid of God
Explanation: He hopes to regain Todd’s faith through fear, but primarily harms Todd and strains family bonds without real benefit.

Event: Ned spends time with Bart in the movie as a better parent figure than Homer
Explanation: He gets emotional fulfillment from mentoring Bart while giving Bart support Homer fails to provide.

Event: Ned knocks out Homer with one punch when relentlessly teased (first time)
Explanation: He gains brief catharsis but risks social and moral fallout while physically harming Homer.

Event: Ned knocks out Homer with one punch when relentlessly teased (second time)
Explanation: By then he stands up for himself, gaining respect/relief at Homer’s expense.

Event: T-shirt cannons are fired and kill Maude
Explanation: They aim to entertain/promote but accidentally cause a needless death with no real gain.

Event: Ned wears a black armband and mourns Edna’s death
Explanation: He suffers emotionally while honoring her and modeling healthy grief for others.

Event: Ned unknowingly treats Homer’s blatant Jerkass comments as well-intentioned advice in “When Flanders Failed”
Explanation: His oblivious optimism exposes him to mockery but encourages Homer’s eventual better behavior.

Event: Ned reports Homer to the police for having sex with Marge in the treehouse
Explanation: He validates his own prudish standards while involving law enforcement over a harmless private act.

Event: Ned drinks at Moe’s to drown his sorrows over Todd’s loss of faith
Explanation: He harms his own health and coping capacity while inconveniencing others at the bar.

Event: Ned repeatedly calls Reverend Lovejoy about trivial sins
Explanation: He soothes his anxieties but overburdens Lovejoy and strains the pastor’s patience.

Event: Ned lets Homer “borrow” his new TV tray indefinitely
Explanation: He loses property and enables exploitation while Homer benefits from free use of the tray.

Event: Ned has a mid-life crisis realizing he wasted his life doing nothing thrilling
Explanation: He suffers acute regret yet gains insight that may push him to change.

Event: Homer helps Ned out of serious financial trouble in “When Flanders Failed”
Explanation: Homer feels better about himself and preserves Ned’s business and livelihood.

Event: Ned sinks all his life savings into the Leftorium, a niche store, trusting God instead of planning
Explanation: He risks and nearly loses his savings but eventually provides a useful service to left-handers.

Event: Ned fails to promote the Leftorium
Explanation: His passivity endangers his finances and delays benefits to potential customers.

Event: Ned doesn’t buy homeowners insurance before “Hurricane Neddy”
Explanation: He suffers massive loss with minimal protection for his family, providing at best a later lesson to others.

Event: Ned snaps in “Hurricane Neddy” and verbally attacks his neighbors
Explanation: He vents pent-up rage but damages relationships and shocks the community.

Event: Ned joins Marge as a confidant when she feels neglected
Explanation: He gains closeness and moral satisfaction while giving Marge emotional support.

Event: Homer confides in Ned about trying to be better
Explanation: Homer gains guidance and relief while Ned feels valued and can encourage positive change.

Event: The townspeople rebuild the Flanders house after the hurricane
Explanation: They gain community pride and gratitude while materially helping Ned’s family recover.

Event: College girls rent Ned’s room and secretly film a “sexy slumber party” webcam show
Explanation: They profit and have fun at his expense, humiliating him and violating his trust.

Event: Homer leaks the “sexy slumber party” video to everyone in town
Explanation: He gains amusement and social capital while causing Ned intense public humiliation.

Event: The townspeople cheer the college girls and mock Ned when he evicts them
Explanation: They enjoy entertainment and shared derision while compounding Ned’s hurt and isolation.

Event: Ned evicts the college girls after discovering the webcam video
Explanation: He protects his home and dignity while abruptly disrupting their housing and project.

Event: Ned takes charge of Springfield’s surveillance system in “To Surveil with Love”
Explanation: He feels empowered and morally superior while invading privacy and breeding resentment.

Event: Ned’s surveillance prompts Bart and Homer to create the “chaos corner”
Explanation: His meddling backfires, worsening disorder without delivering real moral improvement.

Event: Ned agrees to help Homer destroy the surveillance cameras after realizing his error
Explanation: He regains integrity and peace of mind while restoring others’ privacy and autonomy.

Event: Ned’s beatnik parents refuse to discipline or pay attention to him
Explanation: They avoid effort but raise a severely maladjusted child, hurting him and ultimately themselves.

Event: Therapists spank young Ned daily for a year to cure his misbehavior
Explanation: They gain payment and a superficial “cure” yet traumatize him and cause long-term psychological issues.

Event: Ned rents out rooms in his house to people in need
Explanation: He gains rent and spiritual satisfaction while providing shelter and support to others.

Event: The college girls and Homer’s video reveal that many townspeople secretly mock Ned
Explanation: They bond over shared contempt and amusement while eroding Ned’s social standing and trust.

Event: Ned becomes a peacemaker in neighborly conflicts and convinces Springfield to give the Simpsons more chances
Explanation: He earns moral authority and friendships while preventing mob overreactions against the Simpsons.

Event: Ned takes Nelson under his wing as a father figure in “Better Off Ned”
Explanation: He gains purpose and fulfillment while giving Nelson guidance and stability.

Event: Ned tells Todd to shoot him if he tries to get back into the bunker in “Bart’s Comet”
Explanation: He dramatizes commitment at the cost of traumatising Todd and implying self-harm.

Event: Ned openly mocks Apu’s Hinduism in “Midnight Rx”
Explanation: He bolsters his own sense of religious superiority while alienating and hurting Apu.

Event: Ned favors Todd over Rod by giving him more tasks and shared activities
Explanation: He might feel closer to Todd but harms Rod’s self-worth and family cohesion.

Event: Ned hates his negligent beatnik parents
Explanation: His resentment pains him yet reasonably condemns their harmful neglect, signaling to others what not to do.

Event: Ned stays cheerful for God and his boys despite losing Maude and Edna
Explanation: He suppresses his own grief while giving his sons stability and hope.

Event: Ned becomes increasingly hostile and fundamentalist after Edna’s death
Explanation: His rigidity may comfort his worldview but isolates him and harms relations with others.

Event: Ned’s self-centered religious crusades disturb fellow Christians like Bode
Explanation: He affirms his zeal while alienating even sympathetic believers.

Event: Ned serves as a wholesome father figure to the Simpson kids
Explanation: He gains emotional reward and purpose while supporting the children where Homer falls short.

Event: Ned ousts the college girls after discovering the webcam video (explicit eviction scene)
Explanation: He reasserts boundaries and safety while abruptly evicting them and ending their scheme.

Event: The townspeople twice beat up Ned when he defends the Simpsons (movie and “At Long Last, Leave”)
Explanation: They vent anger and enforce conformity but damage a loyal neighbor and gain no lasting benefit.

Event: Ned stops taking crap from Homer by “The Squirt and the Whale”
Explanation: He protects his dignity and reduces being exploited while occasionally pushing back at Homer’s expense.

Event: Ned demands respect for his own religion while mocking others in later seasons
Explanation: He gets self-satisfaction and a sense of righteousness while degrading interfaith relations.

Event: Ned turns the other cheek and forgives Homer’s antagonism for years
Explanation: He tolerates mistreatment but helps Homer keep a friend and sometimes learn to do better.

Event: Homer saves Ned from financial ruin in “When Flanders Failed” (explicit rescue)
Explanation: Homer gains redemption and self-respect while preserving Ned’s home and livelihood.

Event: Ned considers Homer a horrible person by “Hurricane Neddy” but still helps him later
Explanation: He continues to invest in someone who hurts him, sacrificing emotional comfort to maintain neighborly support.

Event: Ned’s sons tell him he needs “a new mommy”
Explanation: They risk upsetting him but try to push him toward companionship and reduced rigidity.

Event: God sends a gust of wind that blows away Bible quotes except one encouraging Ned to have sex with Sara Sloane
Explanation: Ned gains intimacy and joy while Sara gets desired affection, with no evident harm.
